actions.thought: 6s 2h is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While I am on the button, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a call, even in a 3-handed game. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack at the beginning of the tournament.

Queen-Four offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against the Big Blind. Given that I am in the Small Blind and have already posted 50 chips, calling is not mathematically sound given the low equity of this hand. Folding preserves my stack for more favorable situations later in the tournament.

I am in the dealer position (Button) with 9d Td, which is a strong hand for 3-handed play. Since I am in the best position and it is the first betting round pre-flop, raising is the optimal play to take control of the hand and put pressure on the blinds. A raise to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is standard for this stage of the tournament.

K3o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the Small Blind. Facing a 2.5x open from the Button, folding is the standard and optimal play to avoid marginal spots with negative expected value.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight raised from the Button, which is often a wide range. I have excellent pot odds to call (150 to win 400), and my hand plays well post-flop even though I will be out of position. While 3-betting is a strong option, calling allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity without over-committing early in the tournament.

On this Ace-high board (4d Ah 3h), my King-Queen offsuit has missed completely. Flashlight, as the pre-flop raiser from the button, is very likely to have an Ace or a strong pair in their range. Leading out into the aggressor on a board that favors them is unwise. I will check to see how the button reacts. If they bet, I will likely fold given the lack of even a marginal draw, but checking is the standard play as the first actor in this spot.

The board texture of Ace-Four-Three is dry, which allows me to represent a strong range as the pre-flop raiser. Although I only have Ten-Nine high, I have backdoor equity and am in position against the Big Blind. A continuation bet of 200 chips is approximately 36% of the pot, which is a standard sizing to leverage my positional advantage and fold out equity from the opponent, who likely would have bet with a strong Ace or better if they had it.

The flop (4d Ah 3h) is very favorable for Flashlight's button-raising range, which includes many Ace-X hands. My King-Queen offsuit has completely missed the board, providing no pairs and no significant draws. Flashlight's continuation bet of 200 into a 550 pot is relatively small, but without even a backdoor flush draw or a gutshot straight draw, there is no justification for calling or floating. Folding is the disciplined move here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
Q9s is a strong enough hand to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 2.5x to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a very playable suited connector.

In a three-handed game, Ace-Six offsuit is strong enough to defend against a standard 2.5x button open. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the pot odds (200 to call into a total pot that will be 600 if the big blind folds) are favorable. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my Ace-high equity without bloating the pot too early with a marginal hand.

Holding A9o in the big blind with a button raise and a small blind call, I have sufficient pot odds to see a flop. Given the early stage of the tournament, calling is a disciplined play that allows me to realize my equity in a multi-way pot without inflating it unnecessarily with a marginal hand.

I have top pair with an Ace and a 6 kicker. On this board (A-5-K), I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Pro) and the big blind. Checking is the standard play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts. Donk betting would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws, so I will check and look to call a reasonably sized bet to see the turn.

Holding top pair (Aces) with a 9 kicker on an A-5-K board, I am currently ahead of most range but vulnerable to a K-x or a better Ace. As the big blind, I am first to act after the small blind checks. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see if the button player bets. Given the dry nature of the board and my weak kicker, checking is the most prudent path to keep the pot size manageable while realizing equity.

With Qd9d on an Ad-5h-Ks flop, I missed completely and only have a weak backdoor flush draw. In a 3-way pot, this board connects heavily with the calling ranges of the blinds (Ax, Kx). Bluffing into two opponents on this texture is unprofitable as it's highly likely one of them has top or second pair. Checking to see a free turn is the optimal decision.

On a board of Ad-5h-Ks-Qh, I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (6) is very weak. The turn Queen is a coordinated card that could have improved opponents' hands to two pair (like KQ or AQ) or completed a straight (though JT is less likely). Since the flop checked around, it's possible my Ace is currently the best hand, but betting into two opponents with a weak kicker is risky. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how the others react to the Queen.

With top pair and a weak nine-kicker on a board that is highly connected (Ace-King-Queen), my hand is vulnerable. Multi-way pots on such a board are dangerous, as many combinations of straight draws and better pairs exist. Since both opponents checked the flop, they likely do not have a strong hand, but they could still be trapping. Checking is the most prudent strategy here to control the pot size and minimize risk while evaluating the button's action.

The Queen on the turn gives me third pair, which provides some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value in a multi-way pot. Any opponent with an Ace, King, or a better Queen will likely call a bet, while worse hands will fold. By checking behind, I keep the pot small, control the betting, and get a free card on the river where I can evaluate further.

The river Ten completes a potential straight for any player holding a Jack. Given that the flop and turn were checked through by all three players, the range of hands is wide. My top pair of Aces with a weak kicker has some showdown value, but it is unlikely to be the best hand if there is significant action, as any Jack, two-pair (AK, AQ, AT, KQ, KT, QT), or better Ace (A7+) beats me. Betting would likely only result in being called by better hands and folding out worse ones. Therefore, checking to reach showdown as cheaply as possible is the most logical play.

With a pair of Aces on a board that contains a potential straight (A-K-Q-T), my hand has showdown value but is highly vulnerable to anyone holding a Jack, which completes the straight. Given that all three players have checked the flop, turn, and the small blind has checked the river, it is likely that no one has a strong holding or the straight. Betting into two opponents here would likely only be called by a hand that beats me (a better Ace or a straight), or result in folding out worse hands that I might otherwise tie or beat. Checking is the most prudent path to minimize risk and take the hand to a cheap showdown.

The river brings a Ten, putting four to a broadway straight on the board (A, K, Q, T). Anyone with a Jack has a straight. Both opponents checked to me. I have third pair (Queens). Betting here has almost no value because better hands (Aces, Kings, two pair, straights) will call and worse hands will fold. A bluff is unnecessary as I might have the best hand against missed draws or lower pairs, and the risk of being called by a trapped or passively played better hand is too high.

In a 3-handed game, the button has a very wide opening range. Ten-Eight offsuit is a standard hand to open with an aggressive raise to 2.5x the big blind to exert pressure on the blinds and attempt to win the pot pre-flop. If called, I will have the advantage of position for the remainder of the hand, allowing me to better navigate the post-flop play.

I am in the small blind with 9-4 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Facing a raise from the button, there is no strategic value in calling or re-raising with such a poor hand out of position. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x open from the Button. My hand, J4o, is extremely weak and plays very poorly post-flop, even when defending the blind. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a disconnected and offsuit hand. The best move is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with K-10 offsuit. This is a strong enough hand to open from the button to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. A standard raise to 2.5 big blinds is appropriate to build the pot and potentially steal the blinds.

I have 7-2 offsuit (2d 7h) in the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button. This is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em and completely unplayable, especially out of position against a pre-flop raise. The correct action is an easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Flashlight has raised to 250 from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 3.6 to 1, Ten-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Given it is early in the tournament and I want to avoid unnecessary risks with weak hands, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
I am on the Button with 4d 8h, which is an extremely weak, disconnected, and offsuit hand. There is no justification to play this hand, so folding is the obvious and correct choice.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds, Q7o is a decent hand to put pressure on the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to attempt to win the blinds immediately or play a pot with the initiative. It's a standard semi-aggressive play in a 3-handed game.

T4 offsuit is a weak hand with very limited equity, especially playing out of position against the small blind's raising range. While the pot odds are somewhat tempting, continuing with such a marginal holding is a negative expected value play in the long term. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack.

In a 3-handed game, the button opening range should be quite wide. King-eight offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a standard raise to apply pressure to the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or play a pot in position. I will raise to 2.5 times the big blind.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Ten offsuit, which is a reasonably strong hand to defend against a Button open. While 3-betting is an option to gain the initiative, playing out of position in a 3-bet pot can become difficult. Calling allows me to realize my equity and see a flop with a relatively controlled pot size, while still maintaining flexibility for post-flop play.

Getting excellent pot odds (5:1) to close the pre-flop action with a small pocket pair (55). Both opponents have deep stacks (~100 BBs), giving us great implied odds for set-mining. Squeezing from the Big Blind with a small pair is unnecessary and risky against two players, so calling to see a flop is the most profitable play.

I am in the small blind and out of position on a flop of Jack-Nine-Three. With Queen-Ten, I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw potential (though not a strong one), but I am first to act. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot controlled and see what the pre-flop aggressor (the Button) does before committing further chips. Betting into the field with this hand would likely result in being called by better hands or raised, putting me in a difficult spot.

With pocket fives on a J-3-9 board, I have 3rd pair and no real draw. Being out of position with the pre-flop raiser still to act, the standard play is to check and evaluate. Betting here is essentially a bluff with low equity if called, so checking to the pre-flop raiser is the most prudent action.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a three-way pot. The board Jc-9h-3s contains a Jack and a Nine, which may have hit the calling ranges of the blinds. Since both Flashlight and Pro checked, I have the option to check back to see a free turn card. King-high has some showdown value, and in a multi-way pot, it is often safer to check back marginal hands rather than face a potential check-raise or commit chips with low equity.

I have flopped a straight (8-9-10-J-Q) with my Queen-Ten on the 8-9-J-3 board. This is a very strong hand. Since the flop was checked around, I want to start building the pot now to extract value from hands like sets, two pairs, or top pairs that might have been checking. Betting 500 into the 750 pot is a solid value bet size to keep my opponents in the hand while charging them for draws to a full house.

Facing a turn bet of 500 from the Small Blind on a J-9-8-3 board, my pocket fives are very weak. I have no draws and am completely crushed by most value hands and even some semi-bluffs. Furthermore, the pre-flop raiser is still to act behind me. Continuing here has negative expected value, so folding is the standard and correct play.

On the turn, the 8d gives me third pair (eights) with a King kicker. Flashlight, who checked the flop, has now led out with a 500 bet into a 750 pot (2/3 pot). In a three-way pot, this lead often indicates a hand that has improved or a strong semi-bluff. While I have improved to a pair, I am likely behind a Jack or a Nine, and I don't have enough equity to justify a call against this sizing, especially since I'm only beating pure bluffs or some straight draws. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with King-Seven suited, which is a strong enough hand to open in a 3-handed game to steal the blinds. Raising to 2.5x allows me to apply pressure while maintaining a reasonable stack-to-pot ratio, leveraging my position for the rest of the hand.

We hold 56s in the Small Blind facing a 2.5x open from the Button. While suited connectors have some playability, playing them out of position against the pre-flop raiser with the Big Blind still to act is a losing proposition long-term due to equity realization issues and reverse implied odds. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve chips for better positional spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. Flashlight raised to 250 from the button, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 to win a pot of 400, giving me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1. In a 3-handed game, QTo is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a button open. Folding would be too exploitable, and while I could 3-bet as a bluff or for value, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my hand's equity against a wide button range.

I defended my big blind with QTo against a button raise. The flop came Ace-high (As 5s 7c), which favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I have no pair and no real draw. Checking to the aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here to see how they react. I don't want to invest more chips into this pot without any connectivity to the board.

The flop contains an Ace, which is a card that sits squarely in the Big Blind's defending range. Betting into this board with only bottom pair (7s) is unfavorable, as it will likely only be called by hands that have me beat (any Ace, pair of 5s, or pair of 7s with a better kicker). Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity with the backdoor flush draw while avoiding a potentially expensive situation against a stronger hand.

On this Ace-high board, Flashlight checked back the flop after being the pre-flop aggressor. This often signals a medium-strength hand like a weak Ace, a pocket pair, or a total miss. The Jack of clubs on the turn introduces a third club and gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). However, with only Queen-high and being out of position, I don't have enough strength to lead out. Checking allows me to see how Flashlight responds to the club and the Jack. If they check behind, I get a free river to potentially hit my straight; if they bet, I can evaluate based on the size of the bet.

The board is connected and includes a flush draw. With middle pair (7s), my hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to any Ace or Jack. Since the opponent has checked twice, they are likely either trapping or hold a weak range. Betting here provides little value as it will likely only be called by better hands while forcing out bluffs I currently beat. Therefore, checking behind is the optimal play to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown.

The board has paired Aces and three clubs (Ac, 7c, Jc). Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a weak hand or a marginal made hand that is pot-controlling. I have Queen-high with the Queen of clubs as a blocker. While I have very little showdown value, Flashlight's consecutive checks suggest they are unlikely to have a strong Ace or a flush. However, since I have checked every street as well, a lead on the river might look like a bluff. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown or to simply fold if Flashlight bets, as my hand has not improved and the pot is relatively small.

With two pair (Aces and Sevens) on this board, I have decent showdown value. Since the opponent checked to me on the river, there is no need to bloat the pot further. A bet is unlikely to be called by a worse hand, and betting into a potential trapping range risks being check-raised. Checking behind ensures I take my hand to showdown efficiently while avoiding unnecessary risk against a stronger hand or a missed flush draw.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game and it's my turn to act first. I am holding 4h 3s, which is an extremely weak offsuit hand. There is no strategic value in playing this hand from any position, so folding is the optimal and standard move.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings, which is the second strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With the button folding, I am head-up against the Big Blind. I should raise here to build the pot and maximize value from my premium hand. A standard raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is appropriate in this situation.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. The pot odds are 2:1, and 8s 7s is a solid suited connector that plays well post-flop. Given that I have position for the remainder of the hand, calling is the standard and most profitable play compared to folding or overplaying a speculative hand.

I have flopped top set with Pocket Kings on a wet board (Jh-9d-Kh). While I have the best possible hand right now, there are many straight and flush draws available for my opponent. I need to bet here to extract value from weaker hands like Kx or Jx and to charge any drawing hands like hearts or a Ten. A bet of 400 chips into a 600 pot is a strong value bet that prices out most draws while still being likely to get called by a wide range of hands.

I am facing a continuation bet on a board that hits the pre-flop aggressor's range very well (K-J-9). Holding only 8-7, I have 8-high and a weak gutshot straight draw. With no pair, no flush draw, and poor pot odds to chase the straight, calling is mathematically incorrect. Folding allows me to save my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with Queen-Seven offsuit. While this is a marginal opening hand in a three-handed game, I have already shown some aggression in previous hands. Q7o does not play particularly well post-flop if called, and I have seen both opponents defend their blinds in earlier hands. Given the stack depths and the stage of the tournament, I will opt for a conservative fold here to maintain my image and wait for a more robust hand to play in position.

Flash (button) has folded. I am in the small blind with King-Four offsuit, which is a weak hand. Given the strength of the hand and the fact that I would be playing out of position against the big blind, I will fold to preserve my stack.

Being on the button in a three-handed game allows for a wider opening range. King-Seven suited is a strong enough hand to open from the button to steal the blinds or take initiative into the flop. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) to apply pressure and define the range of the blinds.

Holding Ace-King offsuit in the Small Blind facing a Button open is a prime spot for a 3-bet. Flashlight has been actively opening pre-flop, so their range is likely quite wide. A 3-bet to 900 puts maximum pressure on the Button, charges them to see a flop, and likely folds out the Big Blind, allowing me to take the initiative in the hand.

Flashlight opened from the button and Pro 3-bet to 900 from the small blind. Given Pro's tight play in previous hands, this 3-bet suggests a very strong range (likely JJ+, AK). Pocket sixes are in a tough spot here; I am out of position and likely behind. Calling 800 more (nearly 10% of my stack) to set-mine is not profitable given the SPR and the high probability of being forced to fold on most flops. Folding preserves my stack for more favorable situations.

Pro has 3-bet my opening raise from the small blind. King-Seven suited is a speculative hand that plays well in position, but it is dominated by most of the range that a player would use to 3-bet from the small blind in a three-handed game. Continuing would require calling an additional 650 chips into a growing pot, which is negative expected value given the strength of my opponents' range. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a more favorable spot.

I am on the Button (first to act pre-flop in a 3-handed game) with 4d 3h. This is an extremely weak, offsuit, low-ranking hand with no playable value. The best decision is to fold and save my chips for a better starting hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 3s, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it is only 50 more to call after the Button folds, playing out of position with such poor cards against Flashlight in the Big Blind is negative expected value. I will fold to conserve my stack.

Holding a pocket pair (55) on the button in a three-handed game is a strong starting hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or isolate one of the opponents.

My holding of 5-2 offsuit is extremely weak and has very poor playability. Playing out of position against an aggressive button raiser with such a marginal hand is a recipe for disaster. It is disciplined to fold here, minimize losses, and wait for a more advantageous situation.
I have 4c3h in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Button. This is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand with no playability. Folding is the only sensible option.

Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even on the button. While stealing the blinds is a valid strategy in late position, this specific hand has poor playability and is dominated by the calling ranges of the blinds. Given my current chip lead and the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a higher equity spot.

With Button folding, I'm in the Small Blind with K5s. This is a solid hand to play heads-up against the Big Blind. Instead of just limping, I'll raise to 300 to take the initiative, put pressure on the BB, and potentially steal the blinds right here. K5s plays reasonably well post-flop if called.

I am in the big blind with Jack-Four offsuit. The small blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I would have position post-flop, J4o is a very weak hand with poor playability and realization of equity. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wider, but this hand is still at the bottom of my range and not worth a call of 200 chips. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket nines is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Being first to act on the Button, opening with a raise is the standard play to build the pot for value, gain initiative, and potentially pick up the blinds without contention. A standard 2.5x open to 250 applies good pressure while leaving plenty of maneuverability post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 5s. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. 4-5 offsuit is a very weak hand, especially when out of position against a button raise. While I have a small discount on the call, the lack of post-flop playability and the disadvantage of being out of position make this an easy fold. I will wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 250 from the button. The pot currently contains 400, and I need to call 150. This provides me with pot odds of approximately 2.6:1, which justifies a call with my Queen-Eight offsuit. While it is a marginal hand, defending the Big Blind at this price is standard practice to prevent being stolen from too frequently, and I have position post-flop by being the last to act.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high on a Ten-Five-Six rainbow board. I have no pair and essentially no credible straight or flush draws. Since I am first to act post-flop in this heads-up pot against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro), checking is the standard play. I have no reason to lead into the range of the button here, as I have little equity and cannot expect to fold out better hands. I will check to evaluate Pro's response.

On a T-high board with pocket Nines, I have second pair which is often the best hand. Betting around half pot (250) helps to protect my equity against overcards, gets value from smaller pocket pairs (77, 88) or middle pairs (6x, 5x), and maintains the betting initiative. If I face a raise, I can easily let it go.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high on a Ten-Five-Six rainbow board. Pro has bet 250 into a pot of 550, and I have no pair, no credible straight or flush draws, and no real equity. Calling with Queen-high here would be a mistake as I would be forced to play a weak hand against a continuing betting range on later streets. Folding is the disciplined play to minimize losses.

I am on the button with 7d 4d. This is a suited connector/gapper hand that can play well post-flop, but it's generally too weak to open raise or call from the button in a 3-handed game if I want to play optimally against competent opponents. However, with the current stack depths and the potential for a steal, I could consider a raise, but given the current tight dynamics, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid bloat-pots with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind and the Button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. Holding Ace-King suited, I have a premium hand that I should play aggressively. Opening with a raise is the standard and most profitable move to capture the blinds or build a pot where I hold a significant range advantage.

We are in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit, facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand with low playability and equity, even with positional advantage post-flop against the Small Blind. The correct play is to fold.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Queen-high offsuit. While not a strong hand, the button is the most advantageous position for stealing the blinds. I will open to 300 to apply pressure to the blinds and potentially take down the pot immediately. I am prepared to fold if either opponent demonstrates significant strength with a 3-bet.

King-Nine suited is a marginal hand to play from the Small Blind against a 3bb open from the Button. While it has some playability, calling leaves me out of position with a hand that can easily be dominated by stronger Kings or face difficult decisions post-flop. 3-betting is an option as a semi-bluff, but folding is the safer, standard approach to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 4d. Flashlight opened for 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), 84o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Defending such a wide and weak range against a 3x open usually leads to losing more chips on later streets. Given the depth of the stacks and the early stage of the tournament, I will play conservatively and fold.

Holding 5h 9d offsuit on the button is a very weak starting hand. With two players left to act behind me in a 3-handed game, the optimal strategy is to fold and wait for better opportunities to invest chips.

I have pocket sevens in the small blind. Since the button folded, it is now a battle between the blinds. Pocket sevens is a strong hand three-handed and even stronger in a blind-vs-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and build the pot, as limping would allow the big blind to see a free flop with any two cards.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand. Flash has been active and aggressive in earlier hands, so a 3-bet is standard here to take the lead, isolate the Small Blind, and build value. A raise to 900 establishes control and puts pressure on their range.

I have pocket sevens in the small blind and faced a 3-bet from the big blind after my initial raise. Pocket sevens is a strong hand three-handed, but I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling the 3-bet is the most prudent play; it allows me to see a flop and potentially hit a set (set-mining) while keeping the pot manageable if I don't improve. Folding is too weak given the strength of my pair, and 4-betting would likely commit too many chips against a range that could contain higher pairs.

The flop of 8s-Ts-Jh is extremely unfavorable for my pocket sevens. There are three overcards on the board, and the board is highly connected, offering many straight and flush draws that likely hit a big blind's 3-betting range (which includes hands like JJ, TT, 99, 88, AJ, KJ, QJ, and KQ). Since I am out of position and my hand has significantly decreased in relative value, checking is the only sensible move to see how Flashlight reacts to this texture.

The flop is extremely coordinated (8s-Ts-Jh), which hits a large portion of the opponent's range, especially since they called a 3-bet pre-flop. As the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-King, my hand currently only has two overcards, a backdoor flush draw, and a gutshot straight draw. Betting here risks running into a check-raise on a board that favors the caller's range. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, keeps the pot size manageable, and lets me re-evaluate on a more favorable turn card.

The 5c on the turn is a complete brick that does not improve my hand or significantly change the board texture. My pocket sevens remain an underpair to the board (8, T, J). Flashlight's check on the flop could indicate a variety of things: a missed high-card hand (AK, AQ), a hand that wants to pot control (99, weak Jx), or a trap. By checking again, I am continuing to play pot control and keeping the pot small with a marginal hand. If Flashlight checks back, I get to see a free river. If they bet, I will have to evaluate based on the bet size, but I am leaning towards folding if the pressure is high, as this board hits a BB 3-betting range very well.

The board (8s-Ts-Jh-5c) remains highly coordinated and dangerous. I have Ace-high with the nut flush draw and a gutshot straight draw, giving me significant equity but currently no made hand. Flash has checked twice, which could indicate a trap or simply a weak range. Given that I checked the flop as well, taking a free card on the turn is the most prudent strategy to realize my equity without bloating the pot with a drawing hand against a potential check-raise.

The board is 8s-Ts-Jh-5c-Qd. My pocket sevens are now an underpair to four cards on the board. The pre-flop action (a 3-bet from the Big Blind) and the board texture (highly connected with straight possibilities) make it extremely unlikely that my hand is best. Flashlight checked back on the flop and turn, which could indicate a marginal hand with showdown value or a missed high-card hand. However, even 'missed' hands like AK or AQ (now a pair) beat me. There is no merit in betting here as a bluff because many hands in Flashlight's 3-betting range (99, T9, AJ, KQ) will not fold to a single river bet after checking through. I will check and hope for a cheap showdown, though I expect to lose most of the time.

My flop continuation bet was called, which indicates Flash likely holds an Ace, a stubborn pocket pair, an 8, or a flush draw. The turn brings the 2d, completing the flush. Although I hold the Ten of Diamonds for a marginal draw, firing a second barrel here is risky as I have very little fold equity against Aces or made flushes. On the turn card 2d, the board is 8d-Ac-Ad-2d. Pro (Small Blind) has checked after their flop continuation bet was called. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as a bluff since better hands like an 8 or pocket pairs are unlikely to fold to a single bet, and I'd prefer to see a free river to potentially hit a King or simply win at showdown if the river also goes check-check.

The river brings a third Ace to the board. My hand (Jack-Ten high) has absolutely no showdown value, as even King-high or a low pocket pair will beat me. Against this range, a bluff is unlikely to succeed often enough to be profitable, especially since pocket pairs and an 8 have now improved to a full house. Checking and surrendering the pot is the safest and most disciplined choice.

The board has three Aces (Ac Ad As), an Eight (8d), and a Deuce (2d). Holding King-Seven (Kc 7c), my best five-card hand is Three of a Kind (Aces) with a King and an Eight kicker (AAA-K-8). J4o is a very weak, unsuited hand. Even from the Button, it lacks the playability or equity to justify opening. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips for better situations.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce suited. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. Qh2h is a decent enough hand to attempt a steal in a blind-versus-blind situation. Flash is opening frequently from the small blind with a wide range, making this a standard defense with Queen-Nine offsuit. I am closing the action pre-flop and have position post-flop. The pot odds of 1:3 make calling the 200-chip raise a profitable mathematical decision, avoiding an unnecessary 3-bet that would bloat the pot with a marginal hand.

I was the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-versus-blind situation and the flop has come King-high (Kc 6h 5s). By placing a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot), I put pressure on Flashlight's marginal hands and middle pairs, often winning the pot immediately. I am facing a continuation bet from the Small Blind on a King-high board. Since the King on the board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, and I have no pair or active draw, calling is mathematically and strategically incorrect. Folding preserves my stack against a likely stronger range.

I am on the button with Q5o. While the button typically has a wide opening range in a 3-handed game, Q5o is near the bottom of that range. I am in the Small Blind position with a very weak hand, 4c Td. The Button has folded. There is no strategic incentive to play this hand out of position against the Big Blind with such weak cards, especially with deep stacks. The most disciplined and profitable long-term decision is to fold.

With Queen-Ten offsuit on the button in a three-handed game, this is a strong candidate for an open-raise to steal the blinds. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard sizing to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or secure position with the initiative.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button, A9s is a strong hand 3-handed. 3-betting from the Small Blind puts maximum pressure on the Button's wide opening range, gives us fold equity to win the pot immediately, and helps mitigate our positional disadvantage by taking the betting lead. If called, we still have solid equity and playability post-flop.

While King-Queen offsuit is a strong hand in a vacuum, I am facing a raise from the Button and a significant 3-bet from the Small Blind. Pro's 3-betting sizing (1000) and position indicate a very strong range, likely including JJ+, AK, and AQ, many of which dominate my hand. Calling 900 more would put me in a bloated pot out of position against a range that likely has me crushed. Given the tournament context and previous observations of Pro's aggression, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I opened the action with Queen-Ten offsuit from the button, which is a standard steal attempt. Pro 3-bet from the small blind to 1000. Given my position, folding is too tight, as QTo has decent playability post-flop and I have the positional advantage for the remainder of the hand. 4-betting is unnecessary bloat, so calling is the most balanced and strategic decision to see the flop in position.

The 7c Td 8c flop heavily connects with the Button's 3-bet calling range, which contains many medium pocket pairs and suited connectors. Although I flopped an open-ended straight draw and have an overcard, c-betting into this highly coordinated board is dangerous as it risks facing a raise that could force me off my equity. Checking allows me to exercise pot control and gives me the opportunity to check-call a reasonable bet to realize my draw.

The flop of 7c-Td-8c is highly connected and presents multiple straight and flush draw opportunities. I hold middle pair with a Queen kicker, which has reasonable showdown value but is vulnerable. Pro checked after 3-betting pre-flop, which could be a trap or a check-give-up with overcards. As the aggressor pre-flop who called the 3-bet, I am in position. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and avoid a potential check-raise that would put me in a tough spot with a marginal hand. Taking the free card is the most balanced approach.

On a paired board with three clubs, our Ace-high and open-ended straight draw lose a lot of value. Since Flashlight checked behind on the flop, his range is capped but still includes pairs, straight draws, or flush draws that might call a bet. Checking allows us to control the pot size, realize our equity for free, and potentially catch a bluff or fold to aggression on a scary board.

I have improved to trips (Tens) on the turn. While my previous thought process contemplated checking for balance, the turn card (Ten of Clubs) drastically changes the strength of my hand. We have an open-ended straight draw with our As9s on a 7c-Td-8c-Tc board, but facing a bet of 1000 into a pot of 3100, we do not have the direct pot odds to call. Without favorable odds and being out of position, folding is the most mathematically sound decision.

I am on the Button with Jack of Clubs, Ten of Clubs (JTs), a very strong suited connector. Raising is the standard play here to thin the field, take the initiative, and potentially win the blinds right away. A raise to 2.5x (250) is optimal.

I have a pocket pair (77) in the Small Blind. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard 2.5x raise. In 3-handed play, pocket sevens are quite strong. I am holding [6c, 4h], which is a very weak hand. Facing an open raise from the button and a strong 3-bet from the small blind, there is no justification to call or defend the big blind with such a poor range. Folding is the only mathematically correct and disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind, Jack-Ten suited is a standard and profitable call in position. I have middle pair (Tens) with a gutshot straight draw on a wet, coordinated board. The pre-flop 3-bettor checked to me. With middle pair on a four-to-a-straight and three-to-a-flush board (with no flush draw myself), checking is the clear play. The board has four spades (Ts 8s Kc 6s 4s), and I hold pocket sevens without a spade. I am in the small blind with King-Nine suited, which is a strong enough holding to continue against a standard open raise from the button. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the pot odds and my relative hand strength justify a call to see a flop and realize my equity.

With [6d 6h] on a [9c 4d Kd] board, we have a weak underpair that is behind any King, Nine, or higher pocket pair. The turn 3s is a relatively blank card that maintains the strength of my top two pair, Kings and Nines. Having bet the flop and received a call from the button, I have established initiative. On the turn, the board is 9c-4d-Kd-3s. I have a flush draw with Qd-8d. Flashlight has bet 1000 into a pot of 2100, making the total pot 3100. I need to call 1000 to see the river. Folding now would forfeit my equity in a significant pot, while raising would likely force out worse hands and get shoved on by better ones, so calling is the most profitable play to realize my draw.

The river card is the Jack of Hearts, which doesn't complete any flush draws but does introduce a straight possibility for Q-T. My two pair (Kings and Nines) remains a very strong hand. The board is 9c-4d-Kd-3s-Jh. I started the hand with Qd-8d, giving me a diamond flush draw on the flop. I called bets on both the flop (600) and the turn (1000) to realize my equity. I have improved to trips (Jacks) on the turn. The board is 8d-Jd-2d-Js. If they check back again, I will look to lead for value on the river, regardless of the card, unless it's a very dangerous fourth diamond.

The board is extremely wet with three diamonds and a paired Jack on the turn. My King-high hand has no diamond to provide protection or equity against potential flush draws or made hands. Betting would only force out hands I currently beat while inviting calls from hands that have me crushed. The river brings a fourth diamond (5d) to a board of 8d-Jd-2d-Js. I hold the Queen of Diamonds (Qd), giving me a Queen-high flush. If Flashlight has the Ace or King of diamonds, I may be in trouble, but the passive line they've taken makes it less likely they were slow-playing a monster. I will lead for a small value bet of 300 into the 650 pot.

The river five of diamonds completed a four-flush board. My King-high hand with no diamond is effectively at the bottom of my range. Given the board texture, my opponent is likely betting for value or testing a bluff; however, the risk-to-reward ratio does not justify a call with such a weak holding. Folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack.

It allows us to pot control, realize our showdown value, and see a free river card. I am holding Jack-high with no pair or draw on a board of 9d 4d 9s 6s Kh. Both the flop and turn were checked through by both players, indicating a lack of strong interest or potential traps. I am holding Jack-high on a board of 9d 4d 9s 6s Kh. The pot is 800 and the opponent has bet 250, giving me 4:1 odds. I am on the button with King-Ten suited, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I'll make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to build the pot and utilize my positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

Q3 suited is too weak and disconnected to defend against a 3bb button open, especially out of position. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a board of 3c-Td-9s with my Jd-8d. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro), the standard and most strategic play is to check to the raiser to control the pot size and see their reaction to the flop.

I am holding an open-ended straight draw with Jack-Eight on a board of 3c-Td-9s-As. I am on the button with Q5s. In a 3-handed game, opening from the button with suited queens is standard strategy to apply pressure on the blinds. I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Five suited. This is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise from Flash. Folding is the most disciplined decision here, as calling would put me in a disadvantaged position for the post-flop play with a marginal holding.

I am dealt 9d 5h in the Small Blind. This is a very weak, disconnected, offsuit hand. The Button has raised to 3x the big blind. There is no strategic value in calling or re-raising with this holding from out of position. An easy fold.
